topic: shrimp disease question — EMS symptoms in neighbor's pond

turns.content: friend i need ask you something. my neighbor pond have big problem. his shrimp dying very fast. maybe 50% in one week. i am worry

That's alarming — 50% mortality in one week is very serious. That speed of die-off usually points to something aggressive like EMS, Early Mortality Syndrome. Can you describe what the dead shrimp look like? Any changes to the color or the hepatopancreas — the orange organ inside the head?
he show me some dead one. body pale, you know. and the liver, the orange part inside the head, it look white and small. very different from healthy one. shrimp stop eating 3 day before they start die

That description matches EMS very closely. The pale shrunken hepatopancreas turning white is the hallmark sign, along with a sudden feeding stop followed by rapid mass die-off. It's caused by specific strains of Vibrio parahaemolyticus bacteria. Very dangerous for any nearby ponds. How close is his pond to yours?
maybe 200 meter away. we dont share water direct but same canal system, you know. water flow from upper canal to both our area. that is what scare me

200 meters with a shared canal system is close enough to be a real risk. EMS bacteria can spread through water. Here's what you should do right now:

First and most important — stop taking any water from the canal. No water exchanges using canal water until this is resolved. Second, monitor your feeding trays very closely. A sudden drop in feed consumption is the earliest warning sign. If feed is clean in 2 hours, you're okay. If it starts taking 3-4 hours, that's your alarm.
i check tray this morning. clean in 2 hour. normal. but EMS can come so fast, you know. i see it happen to other farmer few year ago. he lose everything in 10 day. whole pond gone

Feeding normally at 2 hours is a strong positive sign right now. But you're right that EMS moves fast once it hits, which is why prevention is everything. Some additional steps you can take:

- Don't share any equipment with your neighbor — nets, buckets, boots, nothing. Bacteria travels on wet surfaces.
- Watch for birds moving between his pond and yours. Herons and egrets can carry bacteria on their feet.
- Lime your pond banks with agricultural lime. It helps suppress bacteria in the soil around the edges.
lime the bank. okay i can do that. i have lime from last season still in storage. what about water quality, friend? anything i should check more?

Yes — check ammonia, pH, and dissolved oxygen daily if you can. Stressed shrimp with poor water quality are much more vulnerable to picking up the bacteria. Keep the beneficial algae healthy — good green water color means the pond ecosystem is working to suppress harmful bacteria naturally.

Also maintain good aeration. Vibrio bacteria thrive in low-oxygen conditions. Keeping dissolved oxygen above 4-5 mg/L makes the environment much less friendly for the pathogen.

aerator running all day and night already. i not take chance, you know. one more thing — should my neighbor report to government? fisheries office? some farmer here afraid government shut them down if they report

I understand the fear, but reporting is actually better for everyone. The fisheries office can send someone to test and confirm whether it's really EMS or something else. They can advise the whole area, not just your neighbor. And if it is EMS, other farmers downstream on the same canal need to know too.

The government usually doesn't shut down individual farmers for a disease outbreak — they're more interested in tracking the spread and helping contain it. Not reporting is riskier because then nobody knows and it spreads silently.
you right. i tell my neighbor that. better to know for sure what it is, you know. maybe it not even EMS. maybe something else. test is important

Exactly — it could be something else entirely. Without a proper test, everyone is just guessing and worrying. A confirmed diagnosis helps your neighbor treat it correctly and helps you know exactly what you're protecting against.
okay friend. i go check my tray again now. i watch very careful next few week. check every morning, every evening. if i see anything wrong, even small thing, i come ask you

Good plan — check trays every feeding, no canal water, lime the banks, keep aerators running, and encourage your neighbor to report. Your ponds are healthy right now and staying vigilant is the best protection you have. Come back anytime, even for small changes. Better to ask early than wait. Good luck!
